item.title: In this town we call home...
item.content:encoded: … Everyone sings the pumpkin song!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Was that part of the anomalous effect or just that she really didn't care about this kid?

My idea there was she wasn't a very good mother.

I was also not expecting the items to be extra weird as well and I'm not sure how those sit with me. I would love to see either the exchange list added to or a few more tests/interactions with the goblin beyond just how it sits before it disappears now.

I was deliberately trying to avoid the "random anomaly generator" trope, and trying to give some insight into this version of goblins.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm concerned at how a woman traded an 8 year old child without much concern. Was that part of the anomalous effect or just that she really didn't care about this kid? I was also not expecting the items to be extra weird as well and I'm not sure how those sit with me. Would love to see either the exchange list added to or a few more tests/interactions with the goblin beyond just how it sits before it disappears now.

item.title: Crosslinked!
item.content:encoded: Per ihp's suggestions in the SCP-1323 crosslink post, I reworked the link to SCP-1323 and added a link to SCP-097.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Probably, it isn't really cheating the goblins though.

You would also need to rediscover the game independently again, with no memories of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I sort of wonder, if this was real, could I "dupe" them by selling the memories of my favorite game or something and getting both some cool magic thing and the ability to play my favorite game blind again?

item.title: Re: Crosslinked!
item.content:encoded: That's the spirit!

item.title: Crosslinked!
item.content:encoded: Added a long overdue wiki-walking link to SCP-1323.
Noting that this just quietly reached a century.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well done, +1

I think I found a small mistake:

<Begin Log, 01/01/2001 0200 hours>

pretty sure that should be 11/01/2001

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Feel free to write them, then!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: where are the appendixes? i really want to read them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As other people have said, the final interview is basically "yep goblins" and doesn't add anything to the article. The list of exchanges could have been much longer and stayed perfectly enjoyable.

For the article as a whole, I like the idea but there's not much that it adds to the basic concept of "faeries do not have the same concept of value as humans." While I understand that it's a tribute to a poem1, this isn't a particularly groundbreaking assertion, even within the context of the Foundationverse. In particular, I'm thinking of The Shadow Market as a thing which takes the idea in a more interesting direction.

I liked it well enough for an upvote because it has goblins and goblins are great, but I doubt that it's something that will stick with me for more than a few hours. No vote.

Footnotes
1. One which I have never read, so there's that

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! That's what I was aiming for.

BTW there's an Bradbury reference hidden in the article that nobody has mentioned yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interviewed: SCP-2523-1 entity with nametag reading "Bobby Goodman".

I think this might be my favorite line. X3
..which is saying something, because I really like this. Its got a Bradbury-esque mix of "whimsy, grimdark, and one-street-over-from-reality" that I can't help but dig.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's nothing like that outside of North America is there?

I've never seen anything quite like that, although the concept was familiar enough. Christmas stores, sometimes.

More generally they call them "pop-up stores" now, and they're terribly fashionable ;)

item.title: Re: Just one thing
item.content:encoded: Yes, Mnemosyne was the Greek goddess of memory. That's where we get the words "amnesia" (a+mnesia = no memory) and "mnemonic". So a drug that makes you remember is a "mnestic" ("mnesia" memory + "tic" ending for medical agents).

item.title: Re: Just one thing
item.content:encoded: There's an "s" in mnestic…

item.title: Re: Just one thing
item.content:encoded: I think he's talking about mnestics.

item.title: Re: Just one thing
item.content:encoded: Mnestics are a thing. Or, they became a thing not too long ago. They do the opposite of amnestics: they help you remember things you'd otherwise forget (like SCP-055).

item.title: Re: Just one thing
item.content:encoded: Where? I did a search just now I can't find this typo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well these are chains ("Spirit" seems to be a big one) that rent empty storefronts in shopping centers and the like, set up a complete retail operation around the very end of September and are gone by the the first week of November (leaving an empty storefront again for rent). They are rarely in the same place the following year, but I guess wherever is cheapest.

There's nothing like that outside of North America is there?

item.title: Just one thing
item.content:encoded:
mnetic

Hnnnnngggggggg.
Otherwise, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, seasons are a foreign concept to me honestly (damn tropics). Though in my country, we have a few businesses opening special attractions for the month of October (in light of Halloween).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mainly I meant these temporary stores, I think they are only an American phenomenon, right?

If not, I need to expand the scope of this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: American culture is quite prominent outside the States in general (and my country may well be the most Westernised Eastern society). I do comprehend the concepts here, though I do have a strong interest in folklores around the world in general.

item.title: Answer
item.content:encoded: I am a non-american, and I get it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now that this has a foreign site translation, I am curious:
Non-North American readers, do you you get what I am talking about with these seasonal Halloween stores?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Significantly improved. Good work.

item.title: Commentary Now Available
item.content:encoded: I've updated Sirpudding's Author Page with commentary for this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I suppose part of it is that for Robin Goodfellow, the distance between the events of A Midsummer's Night's Dream is a long walk, and here isn't significantly different if he goes there and comes back here. Nothing changes in the Land of Youth, ever.

On the other hand it is probably laziness on my part, and I will try to be mindful of it in the future, but at a 50+ rating (and climbing), I am hesitant to make radical structural changes now. :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Meh, ask Kalinin for advice, he's probably got more of a handle on it.

I suppose it is a bit of "Why must magic things always talk in riddles? Why can't they sound like a Brooklyn truck driver?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Traditionally, Samhain is celebrated from sunset on 31 October to sunset on 1 November.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
from sunset on October 31st to sunset on November 1st.

Is that correct? Seems like it should be sunset to sunrise. Also, this just didn't interest me, and some of the exchanges just ruined it further for me to downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
• Who is going into a seasonal Halloween store in the wee hours of the morning of the day after Halloween?
  • I imagine people who use often have drugs on them when they are doing other things.
  • Or it was a test, and the Foundation gave the D-Class the drugs to trade.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Who's going Halloween shopping with 30 grams of smack?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
at summer's end the gates of the border country are opened wide

This was supposed to connect the pre-Christian Gaelic belief that on Samhain ("summer's end") the gateways to the faerie Otherworld are opened with this phenomenon. It is the thing that he is saying we have forgotten (and therefore forgotten how to protect ourselves). I'll consider rewriting it but I don't want to include the word "Samhain" if I can help it.

I can't decide if "places in the shadow that cannot be reached from the now" qualifies or not. It really rides the border between "clever" and "cutesy", possibly because the actual words are goofy, but the concept, another understanding of time, is great.

I am not sure what is goofy about it. Would you have preferred if Robin Goodfellow talked like a contemporary teenager? I thought about this, but thought it would go to far the other way and make it too flip.

"October country" is definitely over the line, I'd say.

Ah, but that is my Bradbury reference! I can cut it. :(

I mean, it's kind of sinister, since he seems to take glee in hurting people, but at the same time, it's a little like those villains whose motivation is "is insane".

It isn't supposed to be glee in hurting people, it is supposed to be an alien mind not understanding how we perceive the world and visa versa. His motivation isn't insanity. His motivation is to sell us things we want and turn a profit on it.

I'm not sure how to rewrite this, but I'm going to think about it.

Edit: The October Country is gone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You humans! Always so forgetful! Such a delight! At summer's end, the gates at the border country are opened wide!

I can't decide if "places in the shadow that cannot be reached from the now" qualifies or not. It really rides the border between "clever" and "cutesy", possibly because the actual words are goofy, but the concept, another understanding of time, is great.

"October country" is definitely over the line, I'd say.

Yes! Of course they are! We are a merry people! Our wares are mirthful!

I mean, it's kind of sinister, since he seems to take glee in hurting people, but at the same time, it's a little like those villains whose motivation is "is insane".

I wouldn't put too much stock in this reply, though, I didn't have such a strong negative reaction the second time around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gotta love the seasonal ones. I particularly like how the goblin didn't care at all about being imprisoned because he knew he would be leaving soon enough. Whole thing is generally charming in an ominous-fae sort of way.

Would be interesting to hear about the Summer and Winter courts from them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well as I said its supposed to have some hints about how this version of faerie works. Notably that time is a spatial dimension to them and that the goblins are a free people who live in the liminal zone between summer and winter. It wasn't meant to be cute or silly, and I'd be interested in identifying the parts that have that effect so I can maybe replace them with wicker man sacrifices and severed heads.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it. Reminds of the King novel Needful Things. My only real gripe with it is that you could probably cut the interview at the end, as it doesn't really add much and kind of sounds a bit silly. Other than that, I like it. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can you give me some examples of where it is too cutesy? I was going for alien and vaguely sinister, honestly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As far as the trades are concerned, my idea here was their trade value in the Otherworld is related to how much the person giving them up valued them here, and that this woman valued her child no more than the addict valued 30 grams of h.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't decide whether I like the Rossetti reference or not. It's a little on the nose for a piece that fits the poem so well thematically - I got that this was a spiritual homage of sorts to Goblin Market (which does give me the warm fuzzies) without it, and it seemed to sap some of the playfulness from the allusion. I wouldn't counsel that you remove it - it's a funny little nod - but it threw me a little and I feel like writing that down. Similarly, the fantasy stuff's a little on-the-nose, and could do with being dialled back a bit.

Otherwise, I quite like it, though the 'memories of a childhood camping trip' as an item of exchange seemed like one of that would sound fresh anywhere else on the internet, but seems a little cliche here. Similarly, I really liked the trades being, by some standard, relatively fair (you shouldn't take some of them, but you can see the logic), which meant that the Sexy Nurse costume costing a missing eight-year-old seems really steep and mean-spirited in comparison with the others.

Mostly, it's a piece that I quite liked, with a few bits that didn't quite work for me. The piece has a sort of playfulness to it that didn't shove itself in my face or try and make me laugh. Wistful, perhaps. It's a tone that I wish more articles carried off. A deserved, but not unreserved, upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I'm put off by the timing. 'Tis the hour of the Cult of Halloween, and I have been sick of their shit since July.

That said, I both do and don't like the interview. I think what I do like is the whole "not Summer or Winter faeries" angle, which is really intriguing and plays out well. The rest, though, I'm probably with Kalinin: too much "cutesy fantasy" talk. Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I remember reading the original. I'm liking this one a lot more. It's seems simpler yet more fleshed out to me.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Goblins are, as I recall, still fae, so souring the milk was a nice touch.

Overall, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I preferred dangerous. It may not have been as accurate without the withdrawal, but it really cemented how nuts the goblins are, and made a nice crisp end to the interview :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I don't know if I can address your more systemic problems but I did address both of your nitpicks, and replaced the compulsions with more interesting effects.

The fact that it can't be interfered with except through indirect means feels like a contrived means of horror.

I think I like writing about a Foundation that doesn't always get to have perfect containment, and that my view of the good folk is that they are very hard to keep imprisoned.

The interview doesn't really work for me. I don't find that it adds much to the narrative, and all it really does when I read it is reinforce this "tra la la oh you delightful human folk" tone that once again sounds like a mischievous faerie from central casting.

Well, it is supposed to be the mischievous fairy from central casting. "Bobby Goodman" was deliberate. The interview was also supposed to convey some things about this version of the faerie otherworld (notably that time is a spatial dimension and that the goblins are a free people that live in the liminal zone between summer and winter). As for the rest, I can only apologize for not entertaining you as he would:

+ show block
– hide block


If we shadows have offended,
Think but this, and all is mended,
That you have but slumber'd here
While these visions did appear.
And this weak and idle theme,
No more yielding but a dream,
Gentles, do not reprehend:
if you pardon, we will mend:
And, as I am an honest Puck,
If we have unearned luck
Now to 'scape the serpent's tongue,
We will make amends ere long;
Else the Puck a liar call;
So, good night unto you all.
Give me your hands, if we be friends,
And Robin shall restore amends.
:)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Couple of nitpicks to start:

These entities behave in a manner consistent with retail employees

This has no real descriptive value.

Exposed persons react to anomalous properties of SCP-2523, including those of trades, entities, and objects originating from the anomaly as though entirely quotidian.

The "entirely quotidian" part seems way too subjective for the context here. Seems like there's a better way to describe the fact that weird shit happening is considered normal by the customers here.

Writing's all right other than those points. My issues:

  • I'm not really into fantasy creatures and concepts actually existing as a starting point. That's not something that I would say is wrong or that people shouldn't do, but it is affecting the portion of my vote that's up to personal preference. I kind of feel like I've seen enough mischievious faeries1 around here.
  • There are so many compulsion effects. People are compelled to not interfere with access to it. The things people buy compel them to do stuff. You're compelled to forget about them. No single instance of this device really sinks this, but taken all together, it runs into the same old issue where the thing does what it does because compulsion rather than any intrinsic qualities.
  • The fact that it can't be interfered with except through indirect means feels like a contrived means of horror.
  • The interview doesn't really work for me. I don't find that it adds much to the narrative, and all it really does when I read it is reinforce this "tra la la oh you delightful human folk" tone that once again sounds like a mischievous faerie from central casting.

Not terrible, but not really moving the needle for me either.

Footnotes
1. Yes, I'm aware they're supposed to be goblins, however they act exactly like the mischievious faeries that are increasingly in evidence on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This made me smile. I believe I may quote this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"This mystical romp of a skip comes fully recommend, for its traditional yet somehow perfect use of fictional and mystic tropes, pitch-perfect delivery, lack of noticeably grammatical errors, and the best use of milk I've seen in a skip so far!"
-Zmax15, The Newspapers

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I've been thinking about trying to write about The Weekly World News in the scipverse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I did not mean
(He did not mean)
To blow your mind
(To blow your mind)
But that shit happens to me
All the tiiiiiiiiiiime
- Tenacious D, Master Exploder

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mean, this could literally lead to a small newspaper article reading: "Woman gets amazing deal on Werewolf Tramsutation Mask for small price of her ability to walk backwards".

Where do you think Clickhole and The Onion are getting their article ideas from?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You sir just blew my mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh. Ohhhhhh. I was about to comment about that too.
Anyway, great skip guys, upvoted!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No. They aren't trying to forget that these are goblins. They are trying to be able to remember that these are goblins. This version of "fairy glamour" is an antimemetic effect.
